pages.text:
xvi
Preface
This seventh edition of Braddom’s Physical Medicine and Rehabili-
tation helps to officially acknowledge that physical medicine and 
rehabilitation (PM&R) has moved boldly into the future of 
health care, embracing all of the potential that technologic and 
intellectual advances can offer, while building off of the 75-year 
formal foundation of the specialty and the millennia of rehabilita-
tive practice across time. Regenerative medicine, virtual care, stem 
cells, genetic engineering, AI-driven diagnostics, brain-computer 
interface, osseointegration, robotics, wearable diagnostics, and 
implantable stimulators are no longer simply within the realm of 
researchers and inventors but are now an integral component of 
PM&R clinicians’ practices. At the same time that these techno -
logic advances are enriching care, PM&R is also increasingly see -
ing a greater acceptance—even resurgence—of person-centric, 
integrative medicine with its whole-health approach to care that 
was an overarching tenet of PM&R before it became popular, 
simply because it has always produced the greatest level of patient 
satisfaction, clinician-patient engagement, and clinical impact 
and best outcomes.
In parallel to this blend of technology and holistic care, there 
has been a highly welcomed renewed focus on physical activity, 
nutrition, restorative sleep, emotional health, mind-body interac-
tions, and other vital elements of health and wellness across the 
lifespan that are leading to further innovation and advances in the 
long-term care of disability. Importantly, PM&R is also increas -
ingly recognizing the value of a personalized approach to care that 
emphasizes the individualization needed to optimize PM&R 
practice for unique populations of individuals at risk for or  
with disability, including those with specific social determinants 
of health, service members, veterans, first responders, women, 
children, elders, athletes, and high-risk workers. All of these im -
provements on PM&R have been in response to individuals living 
longer, demanding more from their bodies and minds, seeking 
more from health care providers, actively engaging in all aspects 
of their health and wellness, and having higher expectations for 
their recovery and functioning than ever before. While prior 
PM&R clinical successes targeted lifesaving or life-lengthening 
discoveries, approaches, and interventions, today’s individuals 
with disabilities and their caregivers are not only expecting to 
survive their acute incident or injury but also to thrive and  
return to an even higher level of living, working, and playing. The 
physiatrist is being asked to balance the holistic approach with 
cutting-edge technology, to combine modern science with old-
world beliefs and practices, and to heal the mind, body, and  
soul. This textbook meets these challenges by bringing together an 
internationally renowned team of authors and associate editors 
from the full range of PM&R practices and systems who have 
created up-to-date, informative, and practical chapters covering 
the breadth of the field. The best of academic medicine, private 
practice, veterans and military health, all aspects of rehabilitative 
services, integrative clinicians, and a wide range of specialty areas 
have been brought together to provide the most up-to-date and 
useful resource for the field.
This updated and expanded seventh edition of Braddom’s 
Physical Medicine and Rehabilitation  remains the foundational 
textbook of PM&R that is an invaluable, practical resource for 
rehabilitation clinicians and trainees and serves as a key reference 
across rehabilitation disciplines by providing traditional core 
written materials supplemented by state-of-the art virtual teach -
ing and training materials. As with the field of PM&R, this 
textbook is being continually reinvented and improved to meet 
the needs of trainees and clinicians as they seek to enhance the 
recoveries, outcomes, and long-term well-being of individuals 
with disability.
David X. Cifu, MD
Editor-in-Chief, Braddom’s Physical Medicine and  
Rehabilitation
Associate Dean of Innovation and System Integration
and Eminent Scholar, Herman J. Flax, MD Professor and
Chair, Department of Physical Medicine and Rehabilitation,
Virginia Commonwealth University School of Medicine,
Richmond, Virginia; Veterans Health Researcher, U.S.
Department of Veterans Affairs, Washington, DC; Principal
Investigator, Long-T erm Impact of Military-Relevant Brain
Injury Consortium (LIMBIC) Central Virginia VA Health Care
System/Virginia Commonwealth University, Richmond,
Virginia
